info.topictitle: A.5.2 Postulates of special relativity (HL only)
info.parenttitle: A.5 Galilean and special relativity (HL only)

markdown:
## The Two Postulates of Special Relativity


In 1905, **Albert Einstein **introduced the **theory of special relativity**, which fundamentally changed our understanding of space and time.

The theory is based on two **postulates**:

<definition term="The first postulate of special relativity">**The First Postulate: The Principle of Relativity**

The laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames.</definition>

<callout type="note">An **inertial reference frame **is one in which an observer is either at rest or moving with a constant velocity (not accelerating). **..**</callout>

> This postulate means that no inertial observer can perform an experiment within their own frame to determine whether they are at rest or moving at constant velocity.

<callout type="example">* Consider that you are in a train moving at a **constant velocity**. 
* If you drop a ball, it falls **straight down**, just as it would if the train were stationary. 
* This is because the ball retains the train’s horizontal velocity when it is released, so it falls straight down relative to you in the train.</callout>

<definition term="The second postulate of special relativity">**The Second Postulate: The Constancy of the Speed of Light**

The speed of light in a vacuum is the same for all observers, regardless of the motion of the light source or the observer.</definition>

> This postulate is revolutionary because it contradicts the idea of absolute rest and absolute motion, even though at everyday speeds its effects are too small to notice.

<callout type="example">* If a car is moving at $60\ \text{km}\,\text{h}^{-1}$ and a ball is thrown forward at $20\ \text{km}\,\text{h}^{-1}$, an observer on the ground would measure the ball’s speed as $80\ \text{km}\,\text{h}^{-1}$.
* However, **light behaves differently**.
* Even if a spaceship is moving at 99% of the speed of light and emits a beam of light, an inertial observer on the spaceship and an approximately inertial observer on Earth will both measure the light’s speed in vacuum as $c \approx 3.00 \times 10^8\,\text{m s}^{-1}$.</callout>

<callout type="note">The constancy of the speed of light was experimentally confirmed by the Michelson-Morley experiment, which **failed to detect any variation** in the speed of light due to Earth’s motion through space.</callout>

## Implications of the Postulates


The two postulates of special relativity lead to profound changes in our understanding of space and time.

### Time Dilation


<definition term="Time dilation">**Time dilation **is the phenomenon where time passes more slowly for an observer in motion relative to a stationary observer.</definition>

This effect is described by the equation:

$$\Delta t = \gamma \Delta t_0$$

where:

1. $\Delta t$ is the time interval measured in a reference frame in which the clock is moving at speed $v$.
2. $\Delta t_0$ is the proper time interval, measured in the rest frame of the clock for which the two events occur at the same position.
3. $\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \frac{v^2}{c^2}}}$ is the Lorentz factor, where $v$ is the relative velocity and $c$ is the speed of light.

<definition term="Proper time">**Proper time **($\Delta t_0$) is the time interval between two events measured in the frame where the events occur at the same location.</definition>

<example_question>
        <question>
        Consider a spaceship traveling at 80% of the speed of light ($0.80c$). If 10 years pass on the spaceship (proper time), how much time passes for an observer on Earth?
        </question>
        <answer>
        * Calculate the Lorentz factor: $$\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (0.80)^2}} = 1.67$$
* Use the time dilation formula: $$\Delta t = \gamma \Delta t_0 = 1.67 \times 10 \ \text{years} = 16.7 \ \text{years}$$
* For the Earth observer, 16.7 years have passed while only 10 years have passed on the spaceship.
        </answer>
      </example_question>

![Illustration of the twin paradox showing that the traveling twin ages less than the Earthbound twin after a relativistic journey.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/cbe71b4e8f9a518c8965f31ee333beed0333c980-1042x1407.png)

### Length Contraction


<definition term="Length contraction">**Length contraction **is the phenomenon where an object in motion appears shorter along the direction of motion to a stationary observer.</definition>

This effect is described by the equation:

$$L = \frac{L_0}{\gamma}$$

where:

1. $L$ is the contracted length measured in a reference frame in which the object is moving at speed $v$.
2. $L_0$ is the proper length (measured in the object’s rest frame).
3. $\gamma$ is the Lorentz factor.

<definition term="Proper length">**Proper length **($L_0$) is the length of an object measured in the frame where the object is at rest.</definition>

<example_question>
        <question>
        A spaceship has a proper length of 100 meters. If it travels at 90% of the speed of light ($0.90c$), what is its length as measured by an observer at rest?
        </question>
        <answer>
        * Calculate the Lorentz factor: $$\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (0.90)^2}} = 2.29$$
* Use the length contraction formula: $$L = \frac{L_0}{\gamma} = \frac{100 \ \text{m}}{2.29} = 43.7 \ \text{m}$$
* The spaceship appears only 43.7 meters long to the stationary observer.
        </answer>
      </example_question>

![Same object shown at rest and at 0.3c, 0.6c, and 0.9c to illustrate length contraction in the direction of motion.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ca734bd7d19ecb0fd9f7f548deb5b28edd80f3b4-1971x873.png)

## Why Do These Effects Matter?


1. These relativistic effects become significant at speeds **close to the speed of light**.
2. At **everyday speeds**, the Lorentz factor ($\gamma$) is approximately 1, so **time dilation** and** length contraction** are **negligible**.
3. However, at **relativistic speeds**, these effects have profound implications for physics and technology.

<callout type="example">#### **Muon decay experiments**


* Muons are unstable particles with a **short lifetime**. 
* When muons are created in the upper atmosphere and travel towards Earth at **near-light speeds**, their lifetime (as measured by Earth observers) is **extended **due to** time dilation**. 
* This allows them to **reach** the surface **before decaying**, providing experimental evidence for relativity.</callout>

## The Ultimate Speed Limit


1. One of the most revolutionary implications of special relativity is that the **speed of light** is the **ultimate speed limit**.
2. No object with mass can **reach **or **exceed** this speed.
3. As an object **approaches **the speed of light, its **energy **and **momentum increase **dramatically, requiring **infinite energy** to reach $c$.

<callout type="note">This limitation has profound implications for our understanding of the universe, including the **behavior of particles** in accelerators and the nature of **black holes**.</callout>
